research_article.title: Practitioner's Guide to Hamstrings
research_article.key_findings: Evidence-based hamstring injury prevention and rehabilitation guide. Nordic curl programs reduce injury risk by 50-70%. Emphasizes eccentric strength development, bilateral symmetry monitoring, and criteria-based return to sport. Previous injury is the strongest risk factor for reinjury.
practical_applications: Implement Nordic curl programs (2-3x per week)

knowledge_base_entries.question: What are common hamstring injuries in athletes and their risk factors?
knowledge_base_entries.answer: Hamstring strains are among the most common injuries in sprinting and field sports, with the biceps femoris long head most frequently affected. Injuries typically occur during high-speed running in the terminal swing phase. Risk factors include previous hamstring injury (strongest predictor), age, inadequate eccentric strength, bilateral asymmetries >15%, fatigue, and insufficient warm-up. Reinjury rates range from 12-30%.
knowledge_base_entries.summary: Hamstring strains commonly affect the biceps femoris during high-speed running. Previous injury is the strongest risk factor.
contraindications: Avoid high-speed running during acute phase
Do not stretch aggressively early in rehabilitation
Avoid premature return to sport before criteria met
Do not ignore persistent pain or tightness
safety_warnings: Previous injury is the strongest risk factor
Premature return to sport leads to reinjury

knowledge_base_entries.question: How should hamstring strength be assessed in athletes?
knowledge_base_entries.answer: Hamstring strength assessment prioritizes eccentric strength measurement using Nordic curl testing, isokinetic dynamometry at various speeds, and isometric testing. Key metrics include peak eccentric force, bilateral asymmetry (<10% ideal), and hamstring:quadriceps ratios (eccentric H:Q >0.8 ideal, conventional H:Q >0.6). Regular testing (preseason and monthly during season) identifies at-risk athletes before injury occurs.
knowledge_base_entries.summary: Hamstring assessment emphasizes eccentric strength via Nordic curls and isokinetics, monitoring asymmetries and H:Q ratios.
best_practices: Use Nordic curl for field-based assessment
Test previously injured athletes more frequently

knowledge_base_entries.question: What are evidence-based hamstring rehabilitation strategies?
knowledge_base_entries.answer: Hamstring rehabilitation follows progressive phases: acute management (POLICE protocol), early mobility and isometric strength, progressive eccentric strengthening (Nordic curls central), running progression (jog to sprint), and return to sport testing. Duration ranges 3-12+ weeks depending on severity. Criteria-based progression (not time-based) prevents reinjury. Key criteria include >90% strength symmetry, pain-free high-speed running, and sport-specific testing.
knowledge_base_entries.summary: Hamstring rehab progresses through phases emphasizing eccentric strength (Nordic curls), with criteria-based return to sport.
best_practices: Progress based on criteria, not time
Ensure pain-free high-speed running before return
Address biomechanical factors and load management

knowledge_base_entries.question: What are evidence-based hamstring injury prevention strategies?
knowledge_base_entries.answer: Nordic curl programs are the cornerstone of hamstring injury prevention, reducing injury risk by 50-70% when performed 2-3x weekly. Combine with proper warm-up protocols, sprint mechanics training, progressive load management, and regular strength monitoring. Address bilateral asymmetries >10% immediately. Previously injured athletes require ongoing prevention programs indefinitely. Prevention is more effective and cost-efficient than treating injuries.
knowledge_base_entries.summary: Nordic curl programs (2-3x/week) reduce hamstring injury risk by 50-70%. Combine with warm-up, mechanics, and load management.
best_practices: Proper progressive warm-up before high-speed running
Sprint mechanics and running technique coaching
Ongoing prevention for previously injured athletes
Combine multiple prevention strategies (not just one)

knowledge_base_entries.question: How should Nordic curl programs be implemented and progressed?
knowledge_base_entries.answer: Nordic curls should be introduced gradually with eccentric-focused progressions. Start with assisted variations (band assistance, partner catch) and progress to bodyweight and then eccentric overload. Frequency of 2-3x per week is optimal. Progress from 1 set of 5 reps to 3 sets of 12 reps over 6-8 weeks. Can be performed as part of warm-up or in strength sessions. Maintain year-round for injury prevention.
knowledge_base_entries.summary: Nordic curls progress from assisted to bodyweight to overload variations, performed 2-3x weekly year-round for prevention.
best_practices: Start with assisted variations for beginners
Monitor for excessive soreness (reduce if needed)
